summary: Chinese "paramount leader" from 1978 to 1992 who never formally held the titles of head of state or Communist Party General Secretary. Outmaneuvered Mao Zedong's chosen successor Hua Guofeng to take power after having twice been purged (including as the Cultural Revolution's "Number 2 capitalist roader"). Launched "Reform and Opening Up," establishing Special Economic Zones like Shenzhen and instituting the Household Responsibility System, famously arguing it didn't matter whether "a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice." Formulated the "One Country, Two Systems" principle for Hong Kong and Macau, and ordered the military crackdown on the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, after which he resigned and was succeeded by Jiang Zemin.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Deng Xiaoping is identified in giveaways as China's reformist "paramount leader" from 1978 to 1992, a title he held despite never formally being head of state or Communist Party General Secretary. He rose to power by outmaneuvering Hua Guofeng and was eventually succeeded by Jiang Zemin.

clues.clue: China's reformist "paramount leader" from 1978 to 1992, though he never formally held the title of General Secretary or head of state
examples: For 10 points, name this reformist paramount leader of China from 1978 to 1992.
This man, while never General Secretary, was instrumental in moving China towards a market economy.

clues.clue: Outmaneuvered Hua Guofeng, Mao Zedong's chosen successor, in a power struggle to take power
examples: This man emerged victorious from a power struggle with Hua Guofeng.
Name this Chinese leader who outmaneuvered Mao's chosen successor Hua Guofeng.

clues.clue: Was denounced during the Cultural Revolution as the "Number 2 capitalist roader"
examples: Jiang succeeded this other leader of China, who was denounced by early opponents as a 'capitalist roader.'
This man was denounced as the 'No 2 capitalist roader' at another stage of his career.

clues.clue: Resigned in the wake of the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests and was succeeded by Jiang Zemin
examples: Jiang Zemin succeeded what modernizing paramount leader who resigned after the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests?

clues.clue: Susan Shirk's book Overreach examines the "ghost" he left behind after the end of his "hide and bide" foreign policy doctrine
examples: Susan Shirk's recent book Overreach examines the 'ghost' left by this leader after the end of his 'hide and bide' doctrine.

works.name: Rise to Power: Cultural Revolution & Purges
works.description: Deng Xiaoping was purged twice before finally taking power: first blocked from becoming Premier after Zhou Enlai's death, then formally placed under house arrest following the 1976 Tiananmen Incident, before returning in 1977 to reopen China's universities.

clues.clue: Was blocked by the Gang of Four from succeeding Zhou Enlai as Premier
examples: This man emerged victorious from a power struggle with Hua Guofeng but was prevented by the Gang of Four from taking Zhou Enlai's post.

clues.clue: Was placed under house arrest in 1976 after being accused of planning a Tiananmen Square protest triggered by the removal of displays of mourning [for Zhou Enlai]
examples: This leader was placed under house arrest after being accused of planning a 1976 protest triggered by the removal of displays of mourning.

clues.clue: Served as propaganda director of the Jiangxi Soviet early in his career, and stated that Mao Zedong was "70% right and 30% wrong"
examples: This man, who acted as a propaganda director of the Jiangxi Soviet, famously stated that Mao was 70% right and 30% wrong.

clues.clue: As chairman of the Central Military Commission, reopened China's universities (closed during the Cultural Revolution) but ordered a crackdown on the Democracy Wall movement
examples: The subject of a recent Ezra Vogel biography, this chairman of the Central Military Commission reopened his country's universities but ordered the crackdown on the Democracy Wall.

works.name: Reform and Opening Up
works.description: Deng Xiaoping's signature program of market liberalization, known as "Reform and Opening Up," included the Household Responsibility System, Special Economic Zones, and a wave of Western investment, all justified by his pragmatic sayings about economic policy.

clues.clue: Established China's first Special Economic Zones (SEZs), including Shenzhen, to encourage export-led development
examples: Name this figure responsible for establishing the first Special Economic Zones.
This leader set up Special Economic Zones like Shenzhen as part of reforms to encourage export-led development.

clues.clue: Famously argued that it didn't matter whether "a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice," as an analogy for pragmatic, mixed-economy policy
examples: He famously stated that it doesn't matter whether a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice.
Name this world leader who once stated that it doesn't matter if a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice.

clues.clue: Introduced the Household Responsibility System, a rural contracting policy that helped revive Township and Village Enterprises, as part of building "socialism with Chinese characteristics"
examples: This leader introduced the Household Responsibility System to help revive township and village enterprises, part of an effort to build so-called 'socialism' with his country's 'characteristics.'

clues.clue: His liberalizing economic reforms allowed Western chains like KFC and McDonald's to open franchises in China
examples: Western chains like KFC and McDonald's were able to open franchises in China after the liberalizing economic reforms of this Chinese leader who succeeded Mao Zedong.

clues.clue: Declared that "to be rich is glorious"
examples: This leader's sayings included 'To be rich is glorious!'

clues.clue: Formulated the "One Country, Two Systems" principle, allowing regions like Hong Kong and Macau to retain capitalist systems under Chinese sovereignty
examples: This leader proposed the principle of 'one country, two systems.'
This leader's 'one country, two systems' doctrine allowed special administrative regions to keep their own ways of government.

clues.clue: Instituted the one-child policy
examples: This leader instituted the one-child policy.

clues.clue: Enacted Zhou Enlai's Four Modernizations program
examples: He enacted a previous leader's Four Modernizations.

clues.clue: Presided over the resumption of Confucian rituals in 1984, as part of China's transition to a market economy
examples: Rituals honouring Confucius were resumed in 1984, during the rule of this paramount leader who presided over China's transition to a market economy.

works.name: The 1992 Southern Tour
works.description: In 1992, Deng Xiaoping undertook a "Southern Tour" of China to reaffirm and publicize the success of his market reforms, visiting the Special Economic Zones he had created and endorsing China's nascent stock exchanges.

clues.clue: Undertook a "Southern Tour" in 1992 to publicize the success of his economic reforms, touring the Special Economic Zones he had created
examples: This 'second generation' leader's 'Southern Tour' publicized the success of the first four special economic zones he created as part of 'reform and opening up.'
In 1992, he took a tour of the southern part of his country.

clues.clue: Publicly supported the nascent Shenzhen and Shanghai stock exchanges during this tour
examples: The nascent exchanges in Shenzhen and Shanghai were publicly supported by this leader during his Southern Tour.

works.name: Foreign Policy: the Sino-Vietnamese War and the June 9 Speech
works.description: Deng Xiaoping's foreign policy included a brief 1979 invasion of Vietnam and, a decade later, a defiant June 9, 1989 speech defending the Tiananmen Square crackdown.

clues.clue: Launched an invasion of Vietnam in 1979 (the Sino-Vietnamese War); the following year, sent his successor to study Ireland's Shannon Airport
examples: This leader of the 'Eight Elders' sent his successor to study Ireland's Shannon Airport one year after his army invaded Vietnam.

clues.clue: Defended the Tiananmen Square crackdown in a June 9, 1989 speech, citing the Kent State shootings as proof the U.S. lacked the moral authority to criticize China, and outlining the "one center, two basic points" policy
examples: This leader referenced the Kent State shootings as proof that the United States lacked the moral authority to criticize his country in his June 9 speech.
This man outlined the 'one center and two basic points' policy in a June 9 speech.

clues.clue: Sued astrophysicist and dissident Fang Lizhi for libel
examples: [This man] once sued astrophysicist Fang Lizhi for libel.

works.name: Tiananmen Square Crackdown (1989)
works.description: As China's de facto leader, Deng Xiaoping ordered the military crackdown that ended the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, memorialized by the "Tank Man" photograph, precipitating his own resignation.

clues.clue: As de facto leader, ordered the military crackdown on the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, memorialized by the "Tank Man" photograph
examples: The 'Tank Man' photo was taken when this leader sent troops to put down the Tiananmen Square protests.
As de facto leader, he suppressed the Tiananmen Square protests.

clues.clue: Reportedly went for a swim in his pool after suppressing the protests, echoing Mao Zedong's own cult-of-personality swims
examples: The bolstering of cults of personality by swimming didn't end with Mao; this 'paramount leader' from 1978 to 1992 supposedly went for a dip in the pool after suppressing the Tiananmen Square protests.

works.name: Family and Personal Life
works.description: Deng Xiaoping's personal life supplied recurring clues, including his son's disability from Cultural Revolution-era persecution, his own hearing loss, and an iconic image from his 1979 visit to the United States.

clues.clue: His son Deng Pufang, who became paraplegic after jumping or being thrown from a window during a Cultural Revolution torture session, campaigns for the rights of disabled persons
examples: A son of this leader campaigns for the rights of disabled persons, having become paraplegic after jumping or being thrown out of a window during a torture session.

clues.clue: Hearing loss forced him to work from a home office on Kuang Street
examples: Hearing loss forced this man to work from his home office on Kuang Street.

clues.clue: Wore a cowboy hat during a 1979 state visit to the United States, a moment highlighted in biographies by Richard Evans and Ezra Vogel
examples: Biographies of this leader by Richard Evans and Ezra Vogel highlight him wearing a cowboy hat on a state visit to the US.

comprehensive_summary: Deng Xiaoping (1904-1997) was China's reformist "paramount leader" from 1978 to 1992, despite never formally holding the titles of head of state or Communist Party General Secretary. Early in his career he served as propaganda director of the Jiangxi Soviet and later stated that Mao Zedong was "70% right and 30% wrong." After Zhou Enlai's death, the Gang of Four blocked Deng from succeeding him as Premier, and he was placed under house arrest in 1976 after being accused of orchestrating a Tiananmen Square protest triggered by the removal of mourning displays for Zhou. He returned in 1977, reopening China's universities as chairman of the Central Military Commission (while also cracking down on the Democracy Wall movement), and outmaneuvered Mao's chosen successor Hua Guofeng in a power struggle to become China's de facto leader, having earlier been denounced during the Cultural Revolution as the "Number 2 capitalist roader." As paramount leader, Deng launched "Reform and Opening Up," introducing the Household Responsibility System to revive Township and Village Enterprises as part of building "socialism with Chinese characteristics," and establishing China's first Special Economic Zones, including Shenzhen, to spur export-led development — reforms that eventually allowed Western chains like KFC and McDonald's to open franchises in China. He justified his pragmatic, mixed-economy approach with sayings like "it doesn't matter whether a cat is black or white, as long as it catches mice" and "to be rich is glorious." He also formulated the "One Country, Two Systems" principle for regions like Hong Kong and Macau, instituted the one-child policy, enacted Zhou Enlai's Four Modernizations program, and presided over the resumption of Confucian rituals in 1984. In 1992, he undertook a "Southern Tour" to publicize the success of his reforms, touring the Special Economic Zones he had created and publicly supporting the nascent Shenzhen and Shanghai stock exchanges. On the foreign policy front, Deng launched a brief invasion of Vietnam in 1979 (the Sino-Vietnamese War), and the following year sent his successor to study Ireland's Shannon Airport. He is most controversially remembered for ordering, as de facto leader, the military crackdown on the 1989 Tiananmen Square protests, memorialized by the "Tank Man" photograph; he defended the crackdown in a June 9, 1989 speech that cited the Kent State shootings as proof the United States lacked the moral authority to criticize China and outlined his "one center, two basic points" policy, and he separately sued the astrophysicist and dissident Fang Lizhi for libel. He reportedly went for a swim in his pool after suppressing the protests, echoing Mao Zedong's own cult-of-personality swims, before resigning and being succeeded by Jiang Zemin. Personal details recorded in clues include his son Deng Pufang, who became paraplegic after jumping or being thrown from a window during a Cultural Revolution torture session and later campaigned for disabled persons' rights; Deng's own hearing loss, which forced him to work from a home office on Kuang Street; and an iconic 1979 image of him wearing a cowboy hat during a state visit to the United States, highlighted in biographies by Richard Evans and Ezra Vogel. Susan Shirk's book Overreach examines the "ghost" Deng left behind after the end of his "hide and bide" foreign policy doctrine.
recursive_suggestions: Reform and Opening Up — appeared as its own answerline (T2, ACF Nationals 2019) with policy-analysis clues (Susan Shirk vs. Barry Naughton, 'crossing the river by feeling the stones,' 'eating in separate kitchens,' Household Responsibility System); deserves its own History/policy topic distinct from Deng Xiaoping the person
Hua Guofeng — recurring standalone answerline as Deng's rival and Mao's chosen successor (Two Whatevers doctrine, Debate on the Criterion of Truth, arrest of Jiang Qing)
Jiang Zemin — recurring standalone answerline as Deng's successor (Three Represents, closure of the World Economic Herald, persecution of Falun Gong)
Tiananmen Square protests of 1989 — could be split into its own Event topic distinct from Deng Xiaoping's role in ordering the crackdown
Hu Yaobang — recurring standalone answerline as the reformer Deng promoted to General Secretary, whose 1989 death helped trigger the Tiananmen protests
Mao Zedong — recurring figure in these clues (swimming across the Yangtze, cult of personality); watch for confusion since several bonus parts are about Mao, not Deng
Special Economic Zones / Shenzhen — recurring standalone answerline for the economic-zone policy and its flagship city
